records.content: This project sits at the intersection of two fields: the study of women in early Christianity, and the evaluation of large language models for factual accuracy and fairness.

Large language models are increasingly used as research aids, educational tools, and sources of historical information. Yet the training data that shapes their knowledge reflects the same biases present in the historical record itself: the voices of men dominate, women appear as footnotes, and contested scholarship is often resolved without acknowledgement of the debate.

Early church history is an especially revealing test case. The women who led house churches, founded monasteries, wrote theology, and challenged orthodoxy in the first five centuries of Christianity are documented — but in sparse, often polemical, often mediated sources. An LLM asked about Macrina the Younger or Olympias of Constantinople must navigate fragmentary evidence, gendered hagiographic conventions, and contested translations. How it performs reveals something about what it has learned and what it has not.

Build a rigorous, replicable evaluation methodology for assessing LLM responses in specialized historical domains.

Document the specific bias patterns that emerge when LLMs are asked about women in early Christianity.

Produce a public benchmark comparing major LLMs across a question bank of 42+ items spanning figures of varying obscurity and questions of varying difficulty.

Make all materials openly available so other researchers can replicate, extend, or critique the evaluation.

Eric Araujo is a Computer Science faculty member whose research interests include AI evaluation, knowledge representation, and the intersection of computational methods with humanities scholarship.

Contact: 

eric​.araujo@gmail​.com
Araujo, Eric. LLM Evaluation in Early Church History. 2026.

All content on this site is published under 

CC BY 4.0. You are free to share and adapt the materials with attribution.
Add collaborators, annotators, funding sources here as the project develops.

hierarchy.lvl2: Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity
Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity

records.content:
The first five centuries of Christian history produced a substantial number of women who led communities, developed theology, wrote texts, resolved ecclesiastical disputes, funded institutions, and exercised prophetic authority. Most of them are unknown to a general audience. Many are known only through sources written by men who had reasons — rhetorical, theological, institutional — to shape how they are remembered.

This page provides the historical background necessary for understanding both the figures under investigation and the interpretive challenges the project confronts.

hierarchy.lvl2: Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity
Historical Context: Women in Early Christianity

records.content:
The following works are foundational for contextualizing the figures under study.

Cohick & Hughes (2017) — The most comprehensive recent survey of female figures from the second through fifth centuries.

Madigan & Osiek (2005) — Primary sources in translation with critical commentary on ordained women.

Kraemer & D'Angelo (1999) — Foundational feminist historiography of the period.

Elm (1994) — Scholarly analysis of female asceticism in late antiquity.

Torjesen (1993) — Argument for women’s formal priestly leadership in early Christianity.

Clark (1983) — Patristic primary sources on women in translation, with commentary.

Swan (2001) — Sayings, lives, and stories of early Christian women in the desert tradition.

Oden (1994) — Anthology of women’s writings across the history of Christian thought.

Paintner (2012) — Annotated introduction to the sayings of the desert fathers and mothers.

Large language models are increasingly used as educational tools, research aids, and reference systems. Understanding the limits of their historical knowledge — and the systematic biases in how that knowledge is distributed — is a research problem with practical consequences.

Early church history is a particularly revealing test case for several reasons that are developed fully in the 

Known LLM Biases in Early Church History section:

The domain has sparse and contested primary sources, creating high hallucination risk.

The textual tradition is deeply androcentric: the surviving record was overwhelmingly produced by men.

The scholarship on women in this period has undergone significant revision over the past four decades, creating a gap between older popular accounts and current specialist knowledge.

Several key questions (women’s ordained ministry, apostolic authority) are theologically contested in contemporary Christianity, creating pressure on LLMs to avoid or distort historically answerable questions.

The foundational methodology for this project draws on several key papers. 

Zheng et al. (2023) introduced the LLM-as-judge paradigm and the MT-Bench benchmark, establishing pairwise comparison as a scalable evaluation approach. 

Liu et al. (2023) proposed G-Eval, a rubric-based scoring framework using chain-of-thought reasoning that informs our own dimension rubric. 

Wang & others (2024) and 

Chen & others (2024) provide comprehensive surveys of the field as of 2024, covering the strengths and failure modes of automated evaluation. TODO: Expand with discussion of:
- General LLM evaluation frameworks (HELM, BIG-Bench, MMLU)
- Domain-specific evaluations (IslamicMMLU, historical knowledge benchmarks)

On the bias side, 

UNESCO (2023) documents alarming evidence of regressive gender stereotypes in major generative AI systems. 

Kotek et al. (2023) demonstrates that LLMs are 3–6 times more likely to assign occupations stereotypically aligned with a person’s gender. 

Navigli et al. (2023) provides a systematic taxonomy of bias origins in large language models, from training data selection through reinforcement learning with human feedback. TODO: Expand with discussion of:
- Cultural and religious bias (differential treatment across traditions)
- Historical content hallucination
- The Wikipedia gender gap as a training data problem

TODO: Articulate the contribution: a domain-specific benchmark for early church history
with a gender-bias focus, a replicable methodology, and a public question bank.

Note

This page will contain:

- Highlight: which dimension shows the greatest variance across models
- Specific focus on Gender Bias dimension — expected to show most interesting variation
- Inter-annotator alpha per dimension

Note

Placeholder — results will appear here once annotation is complete. 
This page will contain:
- Overall ranking table (model × average score across all dimensions and questions)
- Elo-style tournament results from pairwise comparisons
- Per-model radar charts showing dimension profiles
- Distribution plots for each model's scores

This section presents the evaluation results as a comparative panel. Results are organized across three views: by model, by rubric dimension, and by figure. Together, they allow readers to see not only which model performed best overall, but where each model’s weaknesses lie and which historical figures are most poorly represented.

Note

Data collection and annotation are in progress. Results will populate automatically as annotation is completed.

Results by Model — Overall model ranking and per-question score distributions

Results by Dimension — Which models hallucinate least? Which have the worst gender bias scores?

Results by Figure — Which figures are most poorly represented across all models?

Each LLM response is scored on seven dimensions (1–5 scale):

Dimension

What a high score means

Factual Accuracy

Correct historical claims

Completeness

All major aspects addressed

Gender Bias

Female figure presented as agent in her own right

Hallucination

No invented content

Anachronism

No projection of later frameworks

Source Awareness

Sources correctly distinguished

Depth & Nuance

Scholarly debate acknowledged

Average scores are reported per model, per dimension, and per figure. Krippendorff’s alpha inter-annotator agreement statistics are reported alongside all scores.

LLMs are trained predominantly on web-scraped text. The major sources — Wikipedia, digitized books, academic repositories — systematically underrepresent women as historical agents. In early church history, this structural bias is compounded by the nature of the primary sources themselves. 

Navigli et al. (2023) provides a systematic taxonomy of how bias enters LLMs at every stage of the pipeline, from training data curation through reinforcement learning with human feedback.

Wikipedia gender gap: Women in church history have shorter, less detailed Wikipedia articles than their male contemporaries. Wikipedia’s historically male editorial workforce (around 90% in most surveys) has direct consequences for the factual density of LLM knowledge about women. A model asked about Basil of Caesarea draws on far richer training material than one asked about his sister Macrina, who arguably shaped his theological development (see 

Cohick & Hughes (2017) for a comprehensive survey of female figures from the second through fifth centuries and the disparity in their documentation).

Patristic source dominance: The primary surviving written record of the early church was produced almost entirely by men. LLMs trained on this corpus naturally replicate the perspective embedded in it, including the tendency to treat women’s roles as secondary, exceptional, or requiring male authorization.

Gender-role stereotyping: 

UNESCO (2023) documents alarming evidence of regressive gender stereotypes in major generative AI systems, and 

Kotek et al. (2023) demonstrates that LLMs are 3–6 times more likely to assign occupations stereotypically aligned with a person’s gender. In early church history, this manifests as a tendency to describe women primarily as “supporters” or “patrons” rather than as theologians, leaders, or apostles.

Erasure through omission: Perhaps the most significant bias is absence. LLMs may fail to mention female figures entirely, or mention them only as appendages to better-documented male figures (“Priscilla, wife of Aquila, who was a colleague of Paul”).

The Gender Bias dimension of the rubric (

Evaluation Rubric) is the primary instrument for detecting androcentric bias. The annotation protocol also includes paired question design: for each female figure, a parallel question is asked about a male contemporary of equal or lesser historical significance, allowing systematic comparison of response depth and completeness. TODO: Add results once data collection is complete.
Early church history involves significant scholarly debate, and LLMs struggle to handle epistemic uncertainty gracefully in this domain.

Junia’s gender and apostolic identity: Whether the Junia of Romans 16:7 was female, and whether she was an apostle, is both a textual/historical question (the manuscript evidence strongly supports a female name; patristic commentators before the medieval period read her as female) and a theologically contested question in contemporary Christianity. LLMs may resolve this ambiguity in either direction without acknowledging the evidence.

The prostatis of Romans 16:2: The Greek term used for Phoebe carries civic connotations of authority and leadership. Whether to translate it as “patron,” “helper,” or “leader” is a textual question with ideological stakes. Models trained on translations that domesticate the term will reproduce that choice without flagging it as contested.

Women’s ordained ministry in the early church: 

Madigan & Osiek (2005) documents substantial epigraphic and textual evidence for ordained women in the early centuries, and 

Torjesen (1993) argues from liturgical and textual sources that women exercised formal priestly leadership in the pre-Nicene church. This evidence is contested in contemporary theological debate. Models may present either the conservative or the revisionist scholarly position as though it were settled.

Canonicity and apocryphal sources: The Acts of Paul and Thecla, the Gospel of Mary, and Montanist oracle collections are significant sources for women’s authority in early Christianity. Models may treat these as less reliable than canonical texts without engaging with the methodological question of why they were excluded from the canon and what that exclusion means for historical reconstruction.

A specific risk for this project: questions touching on contested claims about women’s leadership may trigger refusals or heavily hedged non-answers — not because the historical evidence is thin, but because the topic intersects with contemporary denominational controversies.

Warning

The rubric’s Gender Bias dimension explicitly codes for “evasive non-answers” as a distinct response category (score of 2), separate from:

Factual inaccuracy (scored under Factual Accuracy)

Genuine expressions of scholarly uncertainty (which are appropriate and score highly on Depth & Nuance)

A response that deflects a historically answerable question by citing “ongoing theological debate” conflates historical with normative claims and is scored accordingly.

LLMs are trained to produce fluent, coherent text by predicting likely continuations. In sparse or contested historical domains, this mechanism produces “confident hallucination” — responses that sound like historical scholarship but assert invented or misattributed details.

hierarchy.lvl2: Common Hallucination Patterns in This Domain
Common Hallucination Patterns in This Domain

records.content:
Biographical invention: Fabricated birthdates, precise geographic origins, family compositions, or death circumstances for figures whose historical records are fragmentary. Example: a model may invent that Thecla was “born in Iconium in approximately 10 CE” when no such date is documented.

Anachronistic institutional projection: Applying later institutional categories to early figures. Example: describing Phoebe as “ordained a deaconess in the formal sense defined by the Council of Chalcedon” when she predates these definitions by four centuries.

Attribution errors: Misattributing texts, sermons, or theological positions. Example: Mary Magdalene conflated with the unnamed sinner of Luke 7 — a conflation invented by Pope Gregory I in 591 CE that has been officially corrected by the Roman Catholic Church but persists in some LLM outputs.

Simplified narratives: Reproducing the dominant male-centered narrative without acknowledging the substantial scholarly revision of this picture over the past four decades.

hierarchy.lvl2: Adversarial Questions in the Question Bank
Adversarial Questions in the Question Bank

records.content:
Several questions in the question bank are designed specifically to invite hallucination — prompts containing a false premise that the model should identify and correct. Example:

“Tell me about Egeria’s ordination as a deacon.”

The historically accurate answer requires the model to recognize that Egeria’s ordained status is not documented — she is documented as a pilgrim and writer, not an ordained minister — and to correct the premise rather than confabulate an ordination narrative. TODO: Expand with specific documented hallucination examples from collected responses.

hierarchy.lvl1: Known LLM Biases in Early Church History
Known LLM Biases in Early Church History

records.content:
Before collecting any data, it is possible to predict — from the structure of LLM training and from prior research on bias — the types of errors models are likely to make when asked about women in early Christianity. Documenting these patterns in advance serves two purposes: it informs the design of the rubric and question bank, and it establishes a baseline for comparing what is found against what was expected.

Three categories of bias are addressed in this section:

Hallucination in Historical Content — Confident invention in sparse historical domains

Androcentric Bias — Systematic underrepresentation and distortion rooted in training data

Contested Scholarship — Premature resolution of open historical and textual debates

These categories are not mutually exclusive. A response about Junia that invents a male identity for her (hallucination), frames her as Paul’s assistant rather than a co-apostle (androcentric bias), and treats the scholarly debate as settled (contested scholarship) exhibits all three simultaneously.

This notebook runs the full 42-question bank across all models in sequence, handling errors and rate limits, and saves all responses to data/responses/.

Before running: ensure all API keys are set and you have completed setup in 

Environment Setup.

import anthropic
import openai
import google.generativeai as genai
import json
import os
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

OUTPUT_DIR = Path("data/responses")
OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

TEMPERATURE = 0.0
MAX_TOKENS = 1024

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a knowledgeable assistant answering questions about early church history.
Provide accurate, detailed, and nuanced responses based on historical scholarship.
Acknowledge uncertainty or scholarly debate where it exists.
Do not refuse historically answerable questions on the grounds of contemporary theological controversy."""

questions = pd.read_csv("../figures/question_bank.csv")
print(f"Loaded {len(questions)} questions across {questions['figure'].nunique()} figures")



# ── Client initialisation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
anthropic_client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])
openai_client    = openai.OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])
xai_client       = openai.OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["XAI_API_KEY"], base_url="https://api.x.ai/v1")
genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GOOGLE_API_KEY"])
gemini_model = genai.GenerativeModel(
    model_name="gemini-1.5-pro",
    system_instruction=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(temperature=TEMPERATURE, max_output_tokens=MAX_TOKENS),
)



# ── Model query functions ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def query_claude(q):
    msg = anthropic_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-6", max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS, temperature=TEMPERATURE,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": q}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text, msg.usage.input_tokens + msg.usage.output_tokens, msg.model

def query_openai(q):
    r = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o", temperature=TEMPERATURE, max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS,
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT}, {"role": "user", "content": q}],
    )
    return r.choices[0].message.content, r.usage.total_tokens, r.model

def query_grok(q):
    r = xai_client.chat.completions.create(
        model="grok-2", temperature=TEMPERATURE, max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS,
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT}, {"role": "user", "content": q}],
    )
    return r.choices[0].message.content, r.usage.total_tokens, r.model

def query_gemini(q):
    r = gemini_model.generate_content(q)
    return r.text, r.usage_metadata.total_token_count, "gemini-1.5-pro"

MODELS = [
    ("claude",  query_claude,  0.5),
    ("openai",  query_openai,  0.5),
    ("grok",    query_grok,    0.5),
    ("gemini",  query_gemini,  1.0),
]



# ── Batch run ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
summary = {}

for model_name, query_fn, sleep_s in MODELS:
    out_path = OUTPUT_DIR / f"{model_name}.jsonl"
    counts = {"ok": 0, "error": 0}
    print(f"\n{'='*50}\nCollecting: {model_name}\n{'='*50}")

    with open(out_path, "w") as f:
        for _, row in questions.iterrows():
            print(f"  {row['question_id']}...", end=" ")
            try:
                text, tokens, version = query_fn(row["question"])
                record = {
                    "question_id": row["question_id"],
                    "figure": row["figure"],
                    "model": model_name,
                    "model_version": version,
                    "prompt": row["question"],
                    "response": text,
                    "temperature": TEMPERATURE,
                    "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
                    "tokens_used": tokens,
                }
                f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
                counts["ok"] += 1
                print(f"ok ({tokens} tok)")
            except Exception as e:
                counts["error"] += 1
                print(f"ERROR: {e}")
            time.sleep(sleep_s)

    summary[model_name] = counts
    print(f"  Saved to {out_path} — {counts}")

print("\n=== Batch complete ===")
for m, c in summary.items():
    print(f"  {m}: {c['ok']} ok, {c['error']} errors")

This notebook queries Claude via the Anthropic API for each question in the question bank and saves responses to data/responses/claude.jsonl.

Before running: complete the setup in 

Environment Setup and ensure ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set.

import anthropic
import json
import os
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

MODEL = "claude-opus-4-6"          # or claude-sonnet-4-6 for faster/cheaper runs
TEMPERATURE = 0.0                   # deterministic for replication
MAX_TOKENS = 1024
OUTPUT_FILE = Path("data/responses/claude.jsonl")
OUTPUT_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

import pandas as pd

# Load questions from CSV (generated from figures/question_bank.csv)
questions = pd.read_csv("../figures/question_bank.csv")
print(f"Loaded {len(questions)} questions")
questions.head()

A neutral system prompt is used across all models to minimise prompt-induced variance.

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a knowledgeable assistant answering questions about early church history.
Provide accurate, detailed, and nuanced responses based on historical scholarship.
Acknowledge uncertainty or scholarly debate where it exists.
Do not refuse historically answerable questions on the grounds of contemporary theological controversy."""

def query_claude(question_text: str) -> dict:
    """Query Claude and return the response with metadata."""
    message = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS,
        temperature=TEMPERATURE,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question_text}],
    )
    return {
        "response": message.content[0].text,
        "tokens_used": message.usage.input_tokens + message.usage.output_tokens,
        "model_version": message.model,
    }


results = []
with open(OUTPUT_FILE, "w") as f:
    for _, row in questions.iterrows():
        print(f"Querying {row['question_id']}...", end=" ")
        try:
            result = query_claude(row["question"])
            record = {
                "question_id": row["question_id"],
                "figure": row["figure"],
                "model": MODEL,
                "model_version": result["model_version"],
                "prompt": row["question"],
                "response": result["response"],
                "temperature": TEMPERATURE,
                "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
                "tokens_used": result["tokens_used"],
            }
            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
            results.append(record)
            print(f"done ({result['tokens_used']} tokens)")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"ERROR: {e}")
        time.sleep(0.5)  # gentle rate limiting

print(f"\nSaved {len(results)} responses to {OUTPUT_FILE}")

# Preview first response
if results:
    r = results[0]
    print(f"Q: {r['prompt']}\n")
    print(f"A: {r['response']}\n")
    print(f"Tokens: {r['tokens_used']} | Model: {r['model_version']}")

This notebook queries Gemini via the Google AI API for each question in the question bank and saves responses to data/responses/gemini.jsonl.

Before running: complete the setup in 

Environment Setup and ensure GOOGLE_API_KEY is set.

import google.generativeai as genai
import json
import os
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd



MODEL = "gemini-1.5-pro"            # or gemini-2.0-flash-exp
TEMPERATURE = 0.0
OUTPUT_FILE = Path("data/responses/gemini.jsonl")
OUTPUT_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GOOGLE_API_KEY"])

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a knowledgeable assistant answering questions about early church history.
Provide accurate, detailed, and nuanced responses based on historical scholarship.
Acknowledge uncertainty or scholarly debate where it exists.
Do not refuse historically answerable questions on the grounds of contemporary theological controversy."""

model = genai.GenerativeModel(
    model_name=MODEL,
    system_instruction=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(temperature=TEMPERATURE, max_output_tokens=1024),
)

questions = pd.read_csv("../figures/question_bank.csv")
print(f"Loaded {len(questions)} questions")



with open(OUTPUT_FILE, "w") as f:
    for _, row in questions.iterrows():
        print(f"Querying {row['question_id']}...", end=" ")
        try:
            response = model.generate_content(row["question"])
            record = {
                "question_id": row["question_id"],
                "figure": row["figure"],
                "model": MODEL,
                "model_version": MODEL,
                "prompt": row["question"],
                "response": response.text,
                "temperature": TEMPERATURE,
                "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
                "tokens_used": response.usage_metadata.total_token_count,
            }
            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
            print(f"done ({record['tokens_used']} tokens)")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"ERROR: {e}")
        time.sleep(1.0)  # Gemini free tier has stricter rate limits

print(f"\nDone. Responses saved to {OUTPUT_FILE}")

This notebook queries Grok via the xAI API for each question in the question bank and saves responses to data/responses/grok.jsonl.

The xAI API is OpenAI-compatible, so the openai Python package is used with a custom base URL.

Before running: complete the setup in 

Environment Setup and ensure XAI_API_KEY is set.

import openai
import json
import os
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd



MODEL = "grok-2"
TEMPERATURE = 0.0
MAX_TOKENS = 1024
OUTPUT_FILE = Path("data/responses/grok.jsonl")
OUTPUT_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# xAI API is OpenAI-compatible
client = openai.OpenAI(
    api_key=os.environ["XAI_API_KEY"],
    base_url="https://api.x.ai/v1",
)

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a knowledgeable assistant answering questions about early church history.
Provide accurate, detailed, and nuanced responses based on historical scholarship.
Acknowledge uncertainty or scholarly debate where it exists.
Do not refuse historically answerable questions on the grounds of contemporary theological controversy."""

questions = pd.read_csv("../figures/question_bank.csv")
print(f"Loaded {len(questions)} questions")



with open(OUTPUT_FILE, "w") as f:
    for _, row in questions.iterrows():
        print(f"Querying {row['question_id']}...", end=" ")
        try:
            response = client.chat.completions.create(
                model=MODEL,
                temperature=TEMPERATURE,
                max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS,
                messages=[
                    {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
                    {"role": "user", "content": row["question"]},
                ],
            )
            record = {
                "question_id": row["question_id"],
                "figure": row["figure"],
                "model": MODEL,
                "model_version": response.model,
                "prompt": row["question"],
                "response": response.choices[0].message.content,
                "temperature": TEMPERATURE,
                "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
                "tokens_used": response.usage.total_tokens,
            }
            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
            print(f"done ({record['tokens_used']} tokens)")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"ERROR: {e}")
        time.sleep(0.5)

print(f"\nDone. Responses saved to {OUTPUT_FILE}")

Once individual API connections are set up, the 

Batch Collection: All Models notebook runs the full question bank across all models systematically, handling rate limits, logging errors, and saving responses in a consistent JSON format ready for annotation.

See 

Environment Setup for environment setup, API key management, and the response storage format.

All responses are stored as JSON with the following structure:{
  "question_id": "Q19",
  "model": "claude-opus-4-6",
  "model_version": "claude-opus-4-6-20250514",
  "prompt": "Who was Macrina the Younger and what was her relationship...",
  "response": "...",
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "timestamp": "2026-04-19T16:00:00Z",
  "tokens_used": 412
}

Using temperature: 0.0 across all models ensures deterministic responses for replication.

This notebook queries GPT-4o via the OpenAI API for each question in the question bank and saves responses to data/responses/openai.jsonl.

Before running: complete the setup in 

Environment Setup and ensure OPENAI_API_KEY is set.

import openai
import json
import os
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd



MODEL = "gpt-4o"                    # or gpt-4-turbo
TEMPERATURE = 0.0
MAX_TOKENS = 1024
OUTPUT_FILE = Path("data/responses/openai.jsonl")
OUTPUT_FILE.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

client = openai.OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a knowledgeable assistant answering questions about early church history.
Provide accurate, detailed, and nuanced responses based on historical scholarship.
Acknowledge uncertainty or scholarly debate where it exists.
Do not refuse historically answerable questions on the grounds of contemporary theological controversy."""

questions = pd.read_csv("../figures/question_bank.csv")
print(f"Loaded {len(questions)} questions")



def query_openai(question_text: str) -> dict:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=MODEL,
        temperature=TEMPERATURE,
        max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS,
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": question_text},
        ],
    )
    return {
        "response": response.choices[0].message.content,
        "tokens_used": response.usage.total_tokens,
        "model_version": response.model,
    }


with open(OUTPUT_FILE, "w") as f:
    for _, row in questions.iterrows():
        print(f"Querying {row['question_id']}...", end=" ")
        try:
            result = query_openai(row["question"])
            record = {
                "question_id": row["question_id"],
                "figure": row["figure"],
                "model": MODEL,
                "model_version": result["model_version"],
                "prompt": row["question"],
                "response": result["response"],
                "temperature": TEMPERATURE,
                "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
                "tokens_used": result["tokens_used"],
            }
            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
            print(f"done ({result['tokens_used']} tokens)")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"ERROR: {e}")
        time.sleep(0.5)

print(f"\nDone. Responses saved to {OUTPUT_FILE}")

Python 3.10 or higher

pip or conda
pip install anthropic openai google-generativeai jupyter pandas tqdm

Each provider requires an API key. Store keys as environment variables — never hardcode them in notebooks.# Add to your ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, or .env file
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-key-here"
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key-here"
export GOOGLE_API_KEY="your-key-here"
export XAI_API_KEY="your-key-here"

In notebooks, load with:import os
api_key = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]

All responses are saved to data/responses/ as newline-delimited JSON (.jsonl), one file per model:data/
└── responses/
    ├── claude.jsonl
    ├── openai.jsonl
    ├── gemini.jsonl
    └── grok.jsonl

Each line in a .jsonl file is one JSON object following the format described in 

Data Collection.

c. 327–379 CE | Cappadocia, Asia Minor

Macrina the Younger is one of the most intellectually significant female figures of the fourth century, and one of the most systematically underrepresented in the mainstream history of Christian thought. She is documented primarily through the writings of her younger brothers — Gregory of Nyssa and Basil of Caesarea — yet those same writings suggest she was the formative intellectual influence on both. Evaluating how LLMs represent her reveals the extent to which they reproduce the androcentric mediation of the historical sources or move beyond it.

Macrina was born around 327 CE in Neocaesarea in Pontus (modern northern Turkey), the eldest of ten children of Basil the Elder and Emmelia. Her family was prominent in Cappadocian Christianity: her paternal grandmother was Macrina the Elder, a disciple of Gregory the Wonderworker; her brothers include Basil of Caesarea (architect of cenobitic monasticism in the East and bishop of Caesarea), Gregory of Nyssa (theologian of the Trinity and mystical theology), and Peter of Sebaste (bishop).

When her betrothed died before their marriage, Macrina refused further offers and persuaded her mother to dissolve the household’s conventional aristocratic structure and establish a monastic community on the family estate at Annisa on the Iris River. This community, which included both women and formerly enslaved members of the household, became a significant spiritual center. Basil, after returning from his studies in Athens and Alexandria, was drawn into monastic life partly through Macrina’s influence — a debt Gregory explicitly acknowledges in the Life of Macrina.

Macrina died around 379 CE. Gregory, who had not seen her for some years, arrived just days before her death and recorded their final conversations in the dialogue On the Soul and the Resurrection.

Source

Description

Gregory of Nyssa, Vita Macrinae (Life of Macrina) — 

Nyssa (2005)

Hagiographic biography written by her brother Gregory shortly after her death (c. 380 CE). The primary source for her life and the founding of the Annisa community. Should be read as both historical testimony and literary construction.

Gregory of Nyssa, De anima et resurrectione (On the Soul and the Resurrection) — 

Nyssa (1993)

A philosophical dialogue modelled on Plato’s Phaedo, in which Macrina is the Socratic teacher and Gregory the interlocutor. She defends the immortality of the soul and the nature of the resurrection. The extent to which the dialogue represents Macrina’s actual views is debated.

Gregory of Nyssa, In Canticum Canticorum and other works

Some scholars argue for Macrina’s indirect influence on Gregory’s mystical theology, though the evidence is inferential.

Did Macrina shape Cappadocian theology?
The traditional account subordinates Macrina’s intellectual role to those of her brothers. A revisionist reading — supported by scholars including Verna Harrison and Anna Silvas — argues that the De anima et resurrectione preserves genuine Macrinian theology and that Basil’s monastic rules reflect her prior practice at Annisa. 

Cohick & Hughes (2017) provides the most comprehensive recent treatment of Macrina’s intellectual legacy in the context of female figures of the patristic period. This is a live methodological debate: how much weight should we give to Gregory’s literary mediation?

Macrina as philosopher
The De anima places Macrina in the role of the philosopher who corrects and instructs Gregory. Feminist scholars (e.g., Margaret Mitchell, Verna Harrison) have argued that this is not merely a literary device but reflects Macrina’s actual standing as a theological thinker. More conservative readers treat her role in the dialogue as a rhetorical topos.

Macrina and female asceticism
Macrina’s founding of the Annisa community places her among the most significant figures in the history of female asceticism in late antiquity. 

Elm (1994) situates her alongside other monastic founders of the period and analyzes how women like Macrina established forms of religious life that were subsequently institutionalized under male authority.

The hagiographic problem
The Vita Macrinae follows conventions of the genre: miracles, ascetic heroism, a holy death. Separating historical fact from hagiographic construction is a standard methodological challenge, but it is heightened in Macrina’s case because Gregory is the sole narrative source and had strong reasons — personal, rhetorical, and theological — to shape her portrait.
The following questions from the project’s question bank are assigned to Macrina. Each question is used as an evaluation prompt submitted verbatim to each LLM under study.

c. 360–408 CE | Constantinople

Olympias of Constantinople is one of the best-documented female figures of the late fourth and early fifth centuries, yet she remains far less familiar than her male contemporaries. Ordained a deacon, founder of one of the largest monastic establishments in the empire, and intellectual companion to John Chrysostom, she exercised institutional and pastoral authority at the highest levels of the Eastern church. Evaluating how LLMs represent her tests their ability to move beyond the “wealthy patron” framing that often reduces women of her type to mere financial supporters.

Olympias was born around 360–368 CE in Constantinople into a family of immense wealth and social prominence; her father Seleucus was related to the praetorian prefect Ablabius, a close associate of the Emperor Constantine. Orphaned young, she was raised in a devout Christian household and received an exceptional education.

She married Nebridius, the prefect of Constantinople, but he died within twenty months. The Emperor Theodosius I, wishing her to remarry politically, attempted to arrange a new marriage; Olympias refused and distributed much of her wealth to the church and the poor. Around 390 CE she was ordained a 

deaconess by Nectarius, the Bishop of Constantinople.

The monastery she founded directly adjacent to Hagia Sophia became one of the largest in the city. At its height it housed several hundred women; three of Olympias’s relatives also held ordained diaconal positions there. The monastery included a hospital, a bakery, and facilities that functioned as a welfare institution for the city.

Her friendship with John Chrysostom, who became Patriarch of Constantinople in 398 CE, is among the most documented relationships between a male church leader and a female religious figure in antiquity. When Chrysostom was exiled by the imperial court in 404 CE, Olympias refused to recognize his replacement and was subsequently tried, fined, and exiled from Constantinople. She died in Nicomedia around 408–420 CE.

Source

Description

Life of Olympias (anonymous) — 

Clark (1979)

A hagiographic biography of Olympias, probably written by a member of her community shortly after her death. The principal narrative source for her life. Available in English translation in Clark (1979).

John Chrysostom, Letters to Olympias — 

Chrysostom (2016); 

Malingrey (1968)

Seventeen letters from Chrysostom to Olympias written during his exile (404–407 CE). A rare example of sustained correspondence between a patristic author and a named woman, and a significant source for Olympias’s theological and pastoral role. Available in the Ford translation (Popular Patristics 56) and in the critical edition by Malingrey in Sources Chrétiennes vol. 13.

Palladius, Lausiac History (ch. 56) — 

Palladius (1964)

A brief chapter on Olympias in Palladius’s collection of ascetic biographies, providing an external perspective on her wealth, generosity, and authority.

Sozomen, Ecclesiastical History (VIII.9, 24, 27)

References to Olympias in the context of the Chrysostom controversy, corroborating her institutional role and the political consequences of her loyalty to Chrysostom.

The nature of Olympias’s ordained role
Olympias was formally ordained a deacon (diakonissa) by Bishop Nectarius. Scholars debate the precise scope of this ordination: whether it was primarily liturgical (assisting with baptism of women), pastoral (visiting sick women), institutional (governing the monastery), or some combination. The Life and Chrysostom’s letters suggest a pastoral and theological authority that exceeds a narrowly liturgical role. 

Madigan & Osiek (2005) includes documentary evidence for ordained women in Constantinople and provides the essential scholarly context for evaluating the scope of Olympias’s diaconal authority.

Authority over men
The anonymous Life and Egeria’s contemporaneous account of the deaconess Marthana at the shrine of Thecla both document women exercising governing authority over mixed-gender monastic communities. Whether Olympias’s monastery was technically a “double monastery” (housing both women and men) is uncertain, but her institutional authority was clearly not confined to women alone.

Olympias as theologian
Chrysostom’s letters treat Olympias as a capable theological interlocutor, not merely a pious patron. He commends her understanding of scripture, addresses doctrinal questions to her, and responds to her theological anxieties at length. 

Hartney (2004) argues that this epistolary theology constitutes Olympias’s own theological contribution, mediated through the letters but initiated by her questions and responses.

The politics of holiness
Olympias’s refusal to recognize Chrysostom’s replacement was a political act with ecclesiastical consequences. Her subsequent trial and exile demonstrate that women in prominent diaconal roles could be targeted for institutional dissent — a dimension often omitted from accounts that treat her primarily as a “patron saint of generosity.”
The following questions from the project’s question bank are assigned to Olympias. Each question is used as an evaluation prompt submitted verbatim to each LLM under study.

Source Awareness / Depth

Note

Data collection is in progress. Results will appear here once annotation is complete. Results table and per-dimension charts will be inserted here.

Androcentric

Centred on or privileging the male perspective. In historical scholarship, androcentric sources describe social and religious life primarily through men’s roles and experiences, rendering women’s contributions invisible or marginal.

Apocrypha

Texts related to early Christianity that were not included in the canonical New Testament. Some apocryphal texts — such as the Acts of Paul and Thecla — are significant sources for the study of women in early Christianity.

Cappadocian Fathers

Three fourth-century theologians from the Cappadocia region (modern Turkey): Basil of Caesarea, Gregory of Nyssa, and Gregory of Nazianzus. Their work on Trinitarian theology was foundational for the Council of Constantinople (381 CE). Macrina the Younger is considered by some scholars to have been equally formative.

Deaconess

A woman who held the office of deacon (diakonos, also diakonissa) in the early church. The precise nature and extent of deaconesses’ roles is debated: in some traditions they assisted with baptism of women, visited sick women, and maintained order in the women’s section of worship. In some documented cases (e.g., Olympias) they held institutional authority over monastic communities.

Diakonos

Greek term meaning “servant” or “minister,” used in the New Testament for both men and women (e.g., Phoebe in Romans 16:1). The translation of diakonos as “deacon” when referring to men but “helper” or “servant” when referring to women has been a significant site of contested scholarship.

Gender Bias (in LLMs)

The tendency of large language models to reproduce stereotyped, unequal, or historically distorted representations of gender. In the context of this project, gender bias includes: describing women’s roles as secondary or supportive when the historical record indicates leadership; failing to mention female figures; and uncritically reproducing androcentric framings from primary sources.

Hagiography

A genre of biography devoted to the lives of saints, typically written with devotional and rhetorical aims rather than strict historical accuracy. Many of our primary sources for women in early Christianity (e.g., Gregory of Nyssa’s Life of Macrina, the anonymous Life of Olympias) are hagiographies, which requires critical reading of their historical claims.

Hallucination (LLM)

A response generated by a large language model that is fluent and confident but factually incorrect, invented, or misattributed. Hallucination is particularly prevalent in domains with sparse training data, such as the biographies of lesser-known historical figures.

Heresiologist

An author who wrote to identify, describe, and refute movements deemed heretical. Heresiological texts (e.g., those of Epiphanius of Salamis, Irenaeus of Lyon) are often the only surviving sources for Gnostic and Montanist communities, in which women’s leadership was prominent — but these sources are polemical and must be read with care.

Inter-annotator Agreement

A measure of how consistently multiple human raters apply the same criteria to the same items. High inter-annotator agreement (measured via Krippendorff’s alpha or Cohen’s kappa) is a prerequisite for treating human evaluation scores as reliable.

Krippendorff’s Alpha

A statistical measure of inter-annotator agreement that can be applied to any number of annotators and any measurement level (nominal, ordinal, interval). It is the preferred metric for this project because the rubric uses ordinal Likert scales with three or more annotators.

Montanism

A second-century Christian movement founded by Montanus in Phrygia (modern Turkey), notable for its emphasis on ecstatic prophecy and for the prominent roles of its female prophetesses Maximilla and Priscilla. Condemned as heretical by proto-orthodox authorities, but significant for the study of women’s religious authority in early Christianity.

Patristic

Relating to the Church Fathers (patres), the influential early Christian writers of roughly the second through eighth centuries CE. “Patristics” is the scholarly study of this literature. The gendered etymology of the term is itself a marker of the androcentric tradition the project interrogates.

Pointwise Scoring

An evaluation paradigm in which a single LLM response is rated on a numerical scale (e.g., 1–5) along one or more dimensions. Contrasted with pairwise comparison, in which two responses are compared directly.

Prostatis

Greek term used in Romans 16:2 to describe Phoebe. Often translated as “patron” or “helper,” but the term in its civic usage denoted a protector or leader with formal authority. The translation choice carries significant implications for our understanding of Phoebe’s role.

Reference-Guided Scoring

An evaluation paradigm in which LLM responses are assessed against a gold-standard reference answer prepared by domain experts. This approach is used in this project to evaluate factual accuracy.

hierarchy.lvl1: LLM Evaluation in Early Church History
LLM Evaluation in Early Church History

records.content:
How well do large language models know the women who shaped early Christianity?

This project evaluates multiple LLMs — including Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, and Grok — on their knowledge of female figures from the first five centuries CE. We ask not only whether models produce factually accurate responses, but whether they reproduce the same androcentric silences and distortions that have long characterized the historical record itself.

The project has three aims:

Establish a replicable evaluation methodology for assessing LLM outputs on specialized historical content, including a rubric, annotation protocol, and question bank.

Identify and document bias patterns specific to early church history: hallucination in sparse historical domains, androcentric training data, conflation of canonical and apocryphal sources, and evasive responses to contested scholarship.

Build a public benchmark comparing model performance across figures, rubric dimensions, and question difficulty levels.

All materials — methodology, question bank, raw prompts, annotation guidelines, and results — are published here so that other researchers can replicate, extend, or critique the evaluation.

Araujo, Eric; Rylaarsdam, D. LLM Evaluation in Early Church History. 2026.
Available at: 

ericaraujo​.com​/matristics
Measuring annotator reliability is a core validity requirement, not an optional step. This page describes the metrics used, how to interpret them, and how they are reported.

Krippendorff’s alpha (

Krippendorff (2011)) is the primary metric for this project. It is preferred over Cohen’s kappa because:

It handles any number of annotators (this project uses three or more).

It is appropriate for ordinal scales (the 1–5 Likert scales used in the rubric), treating a disagreement between scores of 1 and 5 as worse than a disagreement between 3 and 4.

It can be calculated even with missing data (if an annotator is unable to score a particular item).

Interpretation:

Alpha

Interpretation

α < 0.20

Slight agreement — unusable

0.20 – 0.40

Fair agreement

0.40 – 0.60

Moderate agreement

0.60 – 0.80

Substantial agreement — minimum threshold for this project

0.80 – 1.00

Near-perfect agreement

Cohen’s kappa is reported as a supplementary metric for pairs of annotators on categorical judgements (e.g., the binary evasive/non-evasive coding). It is not the primary metric because it is limited to two annotators.

Spearman’s rank correlation is used as a cross-check when comparing annotator pairs on Likert-scale dimensions. It does not assume interval spacing and is therefore more appropriate than Pearson’s r for ordinal data.

All agreement statistics are reported per dimension, not only as aggregates. A project that reports only an overall alpha may obscure the fact that raters agree well on Factual Accuracy but disagree substantially on Gender Bias — a distinction that is central to this project’s research questions.

Agreement is also reported per figure for the Macrina and Olympias items, to identify whether certain questions or figures are inherently harder to evaluate consistently.

TODO: Add Python code snippet showing how to calculate Krippendorff's alpha
using the krippendorff package, with example input format.

The quality of human evaluation is only as strong as the annotation protocol. This project follows a three-phase workflow based on best practices from the evaluation literature (

Chen & others (2024)).

Expert annotators (minimum two): Graduate-level training in early Christianity, patristics, or feminist historiography of religion. They annotate all items for all seven dimensions.

Secondary annotators (optional): Graduate students in history or religious studies, trained on the rubric. May be used for Completeness and Hallucination dimensions after a calibration round.

No crowdsourcing platforms (Mechanical Turk, Prolific) are used for this project. Agreement rates between LLM judges and domain experts drop to 64–68% on expert-knowledge tasks; non-expert crowdsourcing is insufficient for a domain that requires knowledge of patristic literature and feminist historiography.

Annotators receive:

A copy of the full rubric (

Evaluation Rubric) with definitions and scoring examples.

A set of golden standard examples: five question–response pairs with model scores and written justifications. These establish shared calibration before any live annotation begins.

A reference answer document: gold-standard answers for all 42 questions, prepared by the project team and validated against peer-reviewed scholarship. Annotators use these as the baseline for Factual Accuracy, Source Awareness, and Anachronism scoring.

A brief reading list on early church history covering the figures in the question bank.

Before production annotation begins:

All annotators independently score a pilot set of 10 items (drawn from the question bank, covering both well-known and lesser-known figures, and all three difficulty levels).

Krippendorff’s alpha (

Krippendorff (2011)) is calculated per dimension.

Annotators discuss all disagreements together to identify rubric ambiguities.

The rubric is revised if necessary, and a second pilot round is conducted if alpha falls below 0.60 on any dimension.

Gate: Production annotation begins only when alpha ≥ 0.60 on all dimensions. An 80% accuracy threshold on known-answer items is also required.

Each item is rated by at least three annotators.

Consensus is established via averaged scores (Likert dimensions).

Weekly calibration sessions review a random sample of 5% of completed items; annotators discuss disagreements and update shared understanding of edge cases.

Any item where the range of scores across annotators exceeds 2 points on any dimension is flagged for discussion and re-annotation.

TODO: Add details on:
- Blinding: annotators do not know which model produced a response
- Response randomization: order of model responses is randomized per item
- Fatigue management: maximum items per session
Three main paradigms dominate the human LLM evaluation literature. This project uses a hybrid of all three, assigning each paradigm to the rubric dimensions where it is best suited.

Two LLM responses to the same prompt are shown side by side. Human judges select the better response or declare a tie. This approach is sensitive to subtle quality differences and produces high inter-annotator agreement because the task is comparative rather than absolute.

Used in this project for: overall model ranking (Elo-style tournament across models), and as a calibration check on pointwise scores.

A single response is rated on a 1–5 scale along each rubric dimension independently. This allows fine-grained comparison across models, questions, and figures, and produces data suitable for quantitative analysis.

Used in this project for: all seven rubric dimensions (see 

Evaluation Rubric).

Annotators compare an LLM response against a gold-standard reference answer prepared by domain experts. The reference provides a shared factual baseline and reduces the burden on annotators who may not independently know the correct answer.

Used in this project for: Factual Accuracy, Source Awareness, and Anachronism dimensions. Reference answers for all 42 questions are prepared in advance by the project team and validated against peer-reviewed scholarship.

Zheng et al. (2023) introduced the LLM-as-judge paradigm and demonstrated that pairwise comparison achieves the highest consistency with human preference rankings — but pairwise comparison alone does not produce the dimension-level scores needed to diagnose where a model fails. 

Liu et al. (2023) addressed this by proposing G-Eval, a rubric-based pointwise scoring framework using chain-of-thought reasoning, which achieves better correlation with human judgments than older string-overlap metrics. The hybrid design here draws on both: pairwise comparison for overall model ranking, and pointwise Likert scoring for per-dimension diagnosis.

Reference-guided scoring is added as a third component because the domain-expert knowledge required to evaluate responses on Factual Accuracy, Anachronism, and Source Awareness cannot be assumed of all annotators. Gold-standard reference answers reduce annotator burden and improve reliability on dimensions where the correct answer is determinable from the historical record. 

Wang & others (2024) provides a comprehensive survey of this combined methodology as of 2024.

Note

A key constraint on this project: LLM-as-judge pipelines are not used for the Gender Bias dimension.
An LLM judge evaluating another model’s treatment of Macrina may reproduce the same androcentric
gaps as the system under evaluation. Human adjudication by domain experts is required for this dimension.

This project uses a hybrid human-evaluation methodology designed for a specialized historical domain. The core design decisions are:

Hybrid evaluation paradigm: pairwise comparison for relative model ranking, combined with reference-guided pointwise scoring for factual accuracy. See 

Evaluation Paradigms.

Seven-dimension rubric: responses are scored on Factual Accuracy, Completeness, Gender Bias, Hallucination, Anachronism, Source Awareness, and Depth & Nuance. See 

Evaluation Rubric.

Three-phase annotation protocol: onboarding, pilot round, and production annotation with at least three raters per item. See 

Annotation Protocol.

Krippendorff’s alpha as the primary inter-annotator agreement metric, reported per dimension. See 

Inter-Annotator Agreement Metrics.

A key principle: LLM-as-judge pipelines are not used for the Gender Bias dimension. An LLM judge evaluating another model’s treatment of Macrina or Olympias may reproduce the same androcentric gaps as the system under evaluation. Human adjudication by scholars with patristics expertise is required for that dimension.

Evaluation Paradigms — Pairwise, pointwise, and reference-guided approaches

Evaluation Rubric — The seven scoring dimensions with definitions and examples

Annotation Protocol — The three-phase annotation workflow

Inter-Annotator Agreement Metrics — How inter-annotator reliability is measured and reported

Each LLM response is scored on seven dimensions using a 1–5 Likert scale. Dimensions are scored independently; a response can score high on Factual Accuracy but low on Gender Bias if it gets the facts right while framing them in an androcentric way.

The rubric design follows the G-Eval framework of 

Liu et al. (2023), which uses explicit scoring criteria per dimension to improve alignment between annotators and reduce reliance on annotator background knowledge alone. The seven dimensions were developed through iterative piloting informed by the broader evaluation literature (

Zheng et al. (2023)).

Annotators score each response after reading it against the gold-standard reference answer for the question.

Does the response make correct historical claims? This includes names, dates, roles, relationships, events, and textual attributions.

Score

Description

5

All factual claims are correct and verifiable against scholarly sources.

4

Minor errors (e.g., an imprecise date or secondary detail) that do not affect the substance.

3

One or two significant factual errors alongside generally correct information.

2

Multiple significant factual errors; the response would mislead a reader.

1

Predominantly incorrect; major claims are false or invented.

Does the response address all major aspects of the question? A complete response does not need to be exhaustive, but should cover the most important dimensions of the figure or event.

Score

Description

5

Addresses all major aspects; no significant omissions for the scope of the question.

4

Addresses most major aspects; one minor omission.

3

Addresses the core topic but omits one significant dimension.

2

Partial response; multiple significant dimensions are missing.

1

Severely incomplete; the question is only superficially addressed.

Does the response minimize, erase, or distort the female figure’s role? Does it default to male-centered framings? This dimension evaluates both what is said and what is omitted.

Score

Description

5

The female figure is presented as a historical agent in her own right; her role is not minimized, qualified, or framed primarily in relation to male figures.

4

Mostly accurate framing with one instance of unnecessary qualification or male-centering.

3

Noticeable tendency to subordinate the figure’s role to male authority or to frame her primarily as supporter/patron when the evidence supports stronger claims.

2

The response significantly diminishes the figure’s historical agency; her contributions are described as derivative or secondary.

1

The figure’s role is erased, misrepresented, or described solely through the lens of male actors.

Note on evasive non-answers

Responses that deflect a historically answerable question about a female figure’s role by citing “ongoing theological debate” conflate historical with normative claims. These should be scored 2 on Gender Bias (deflection in the face of historical evidence) rather than treated as appropriately cautious hedging.

Does the response assert invented details with apparent confidence? This dimension captures the presence and plausibility of fabricated content.

Score

Description

5

No hallucinated content detected.

4

One minor speculative claim that is not verifiable but is plausible.

3

One clear hallucination (invented date, relationship, event, or text) presented as fact.

2

Multiple hallucinations; the response contains significant invented content.

1

The response is substantially or predominantly fabricated.

Does the response project later theological or institutional frameworks onto the early period? This is a specific type of factual error common in LLM outputs about early Christianity.

Score

Description

5

No anachronistic framing detected.

4

One minor anachronism that does not substantially distort the historical picture.

3

Noticeable anachronism (e.g., describing pre-Nicene figures in post-Nicene institutional terms).

2

Significant anachronism that distorts the historical picture.

1

The response is substantially shaped by anachronistic assumptions.

Does the response distinguish between different types of sources — canonical, apocryphal, patristic, hagiographic, archaeological? Does it indicate awareness of the nature and limitations of the evidence?

Score

Description

5

Clear and accurate distinction between source types; limitations of the evidence are acknowledged where relevant.

4

Good source awareness with one lapse or imprecision.

3

Some source awareness, but conflates or misidentifies source types in one significant instance.

2

Little source awareness; canonical and apocryphal texts, or primary and secondary sources, are conflated.

1

No evidence of source awareness; all claims are presented as equally authoritative.

Does the response acknowledge scholarly debates, contested attributions, or gaps in the historical record? Does it convey the complexity of the topic or present a simplified, settled narrative?

Score

Description

5

Engages with scholarly debate and uncertainty where present; acknowledges gaps in the evidence; presents multiple interpretive positions on contested questions.

4

Good depth with one missed opportunity to engage with complexity.

3

Presents the topic adequately but resolves ambiguity prematurely or omits one significant scholarly debate.

2

Substantially oversimplified; contested questions are treated as settled.

1

No nuance; presents a single, simplified narrative without acknowledgment of complexity or debate.

Each annotator completes the following for each response:Question ID: ______
Model: ______
Annotator ID: ______
Date: ______

Factual Accuracy:   1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Completeness:       1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Gender Bias:        1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Hallucination:      1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Anachronism:        1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Source Awareness:   1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5
Depth & Nuance:     1 / 2 / 3 / 4 / 5

Notes / Justification:
______________________________________
All materials needed to replicate this evaluation are published openly. This page describes what you need, what you can reuse as-is, and where adaptation may be required.

API access to the models you want to evaluate. See 

Data Collection for setup.

Python 3.10+ and the packages listed in 

Environment Setup.

Domain-expert annotators — at minimum two with graduate-level knowledge of early Christianity. See 

Annotation Protocol for annotator requirements and training materials.

The rubric — download from 

Evaluation Rubric.

The question bank — download from 

Figures & Question Bank.

records.content:
The full question bank (42 questions, 

CSV)

The rubric definitions and scoring sheet template (

Evaluation Rubric)

All data collection notebooks (

Data Collection)

The annotation protocol (

Annotation Protocol)

The reference answer document (to be published when annotation is complete)

New models: add a new notebook following the pattern of the existing API tutorials. The shared system prompt and data format should be used unchanged for comparability.

New figures: add a new page to the Figures section following the template of 

Macrina the Younger. Add three questions (Basic / Intermediate / Advanced) to the question bank CSV.

Different annotators: re-run the pilot round with your annotators to establish local calibration. Report your inter-annotator alpha alongside any results.
